insights.insight: Ad tech charges $5–$150 CPA to infer a purchase may have happened; a cryptographically verified receipt confirms it did, at roughly $0.10 per signal — a 50–200x cost delta that isn't a product improvement, it's a different category of evidence.

With Chrome deprecating cookies and fingerprinting under regulatory pressure, the ad industry's primary cross-site tracking primitives are gone — this protocol produces identity-free, cross-ecosystem purchase verification that no first-party data program or data broker currently offers.

Amazon knows Amazon spend; Kroger knows Kroger spend; neither sees the other — a system that aggregates verified purchase signals across retailers without holding identity gives brands a cross-ecosystem view that no single platform can sell them.

The network's geographic footprint is building through non-metro markets — 15+ rural state designations including first Mountain West entry — capturing spend from populations that are systematically underrepresented in cookie-based and first-party data programs.

Top-brand data shows verified receipts spanning grocery (King Soopers, Kroger, H-E-B), mass retail (Walmart, Target), warehouse (Costco), and home improvement (Home Depot) — the dataset is not a grocery panel, it is a cross-category commerce signal.

insights.topic: Velocity plateau as intake quality indicator
insights.insight: Token velocity has held flat at 0.579–0.628x for six consecutive measurement periods while geographic coverage keeps expanding — intake is consistent but not yet compounding, meaning the dataset is stable and growing but not yet at the scale where trend signals are statistically robust for campaign planning.
